beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A 50-turn computer-use task. 35 of those turns send the exact same screenshot. Every one re-tokenized. That is 70,000 tokens you paid for twice — or ten times. What if you only tokenized it once?

props.command: My computer-use agent re-sends an identical screenshot 35 times in a 50-turn task — add prompt caching and show me the token savings.

beats.narration_text: Every agent turn takes a screenshot. Most of the time the desktop has not changed. A form is still open. A progress bar is still moving. The agent sends the identical image anyway — and the API tokenizes it again from scratch. 2000 tokens times 50 turns. Most of it wasted.

props.command:
50 turns × 2000 tokens/screenshot = 100,000 tokens total.
35 of those turns: identical screenshot. 70,000 tokens re-tokenized.

viz.description: A horizontal timeline of 50 turns on cream stage. Each turn is a small rectangle. 5 are labeled with screen state letters (A, B, C, D, E) in terracotta. 45 are duplicates in INK_SOFT. A running token counter climbs: 2000, 4000, 6000... to 100,000. The wasted tokens (70,000) are highlighted with a terracotta brace and label. Source: Claude Quickstarts (Anthropic).

beats.narration_text: The fix is one field: cache underscore control equals the type ephemeral. On the first turn, hash the screenshot and send it with that flag. The API caches it. On subsequent turns with the same image, the API returns a cache hit — instant, zero input tokens. You send the image once; the cache does the rest.

beats.act: centerpiece — the filmstrip with cache hits and misses
beats.narration_text: Here is the 50-turn task with caching on. Five unique desktop states: A through E. Each first visit is a cache miss — 2000 tokens. Every revisit is a cache hit — near zero tokens. Total: five unique states times 2000 equals 10,000 tokens. Without caching: 100,000. The savings are 90 percent. Source: Claude Quickstarts, Anthropic.

props.command:
5 unique states × 2000 tokens = 10,000 total
50 turns × 2000 tokens = 100,000 without caching
Savings: 90,000 tokens (90%)

viz.description: A 50-frame filmstrip on cream stage. Each frame is a small rectangle in a horizontal scrolling row. First occurrence of each unique state (A,B,C,D,E) is in terracotta with label 'MISS ~2000 tokens'. All subsequent identical frames are in INK_SOFT with label 'HIT ~0 tokens'. Below the filmstrip: two token counters — 'Without caching: 100,000' in INK and 'With caching: 10,000' in terracotta. A large '90% saved' badge in terracotta. Source: Claude Quickstarts (Anthropic).

beats.act: honesty beat — what this does NOT cover
beats.narration_text: What this does not cover: the full caching protocol — token counting rules, minimum cacheable token thresholds, or cache eviction policies. The cache persists for a session; it does not persist across API keys or long idle gaps. And this is ephemeral caching: it is not the persistent cache tier. This video is the screenshot-caching case only.

props.command: Exclusions: full caching protocol, minimum token thresholds, cache eviction, persistent cache tier. This: ephemeral screenshot caching only.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Computer-use agents re-send the same screenshots over and over. The API re-tokenizes every one unless you tell it not to. One field — cache underscore control ephemeral — flips the behavior. The API caches on first sight, returns a hit on every repeat. In a 50-turn task with 5 unique desktop states, that is 10,000 tokens instead of 100,000.

artifactLines: Computer-use agents re-send identical screenshots every turn — the API re-tokenizes each one by default.
Adding cache_control={"type": "ephemeral"} on first send caches the image; every subsequent identical send is a cache hit (~0 tokens).
In a 50-turn task with 5 unique desktop states: 10,000 tokens with caching vs. 100,000 without — a 90% reduction.
The cache persists for the session; it does not cross API key boundaries or survive long idle gaps.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The prompt: 'My computer-use agent re-sends an identical screenshot 35 times in a 50-turn task — add prompt caching and show me the token savings.' That prompt asks Claude to write the caching wrapper. Does it hash the screenshot first? Does it handle partial matches — screenshots that changed only slightly? Run it and find out. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: My computer-use agent re-sends an identical screenshot 35 times in a 50-turn task — add prompt caching and show me the token savings.
